content.text: LangSmith Monitoring gives you production visibility into your LLM application without adding instrumentation code beyond the tracing environment variables. Once tracing is enabled, the Monitoring dashboard aggregates latency, token usage, cost estimates, and error rates across all runs in a project — broken down by time window, model, and tag.

attrs.alt: LangSmith monitoring: latency, token usage, cost, and error rate feed into the monitoring dashboard with alerting and programmatic query options

content.text: Open any project in LangSmith and click the Monitoring tab to see time-series charts for the four core signals. Each chart defaults to the last 24 hours and can be zoomed to any window. Clicking a point on a chart filters the run list below to exactly those runs — the fastest path to a failing run at a spike.
Latency: p50/p95/p99 for all chain runs, or filtered by node name or tag.
Token usage: prompt and completion tokens per run, aggregated by model.
Cost: estimated dollar cost using each model's published per-token rate.
Error rate: percentage of runs that raised an exception, grouped by error type.
Feedback: mean score for any feedback key (e.g. user_rating, exact_match) over time.
method lets you pull any slice of production data into Python for custom aggregation, export to Grafana, or offline analysis. Use the
parameter with LangSmith's filter expression language to select by run type, status, tag, feedback score, or time range.

from langsmith import Client
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone

client = Client()
cutoff = datetime.now(timezone.utc) - timedelta(hours=24)

# All failed chain runs in the last 24 hours
failed = list(client.list_runs(
    project_name="production",
    run_type="chain",
    error="is not null",   # only runs that raised an exception
    start_time=cutoff,
))
print(f"Failed runs (24h): {len(failed)}")
for r in failed[:5]:
    print(f"  {r.id}  error={str(r.error)[:80]}")

# All LLM runs with high latency (> 5 seconds)
slow = list(client.list_runs(
    project_name="production",
    run_type="llm",
    start_time=cutoff,
))
slow_runs = [r for r in slow
             if r.end_time and r.start_time
             and (r.end_time - r.start_time).total_seconds() > 5]
print(f"Slow LLM calls (>5s): {len(slow_runs)}")

from langsmith import Client
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone
import statistics

client = Client()
cutoff = datetime.now(timezone.utc) - timedelta(hours=24)

runs = list(client.list_runs(
    project_name="production",
    run_type="llm",
    start_time=cutoff,
))

# Latency percentiles
durations = [
    (r.end_time - r.start_time).total_seconds()
    for r in runs if r.end_time and r.start_time
]
if durations:
    durations.sort()
    n = len(durations)
    p50 = durations[n // 2]
    p95 = durations[int(n * 0.95)]
    print(f"Latency p50={p50:.2f}s  p95={p95:.2f}s  n={n}")

# Token usage by model
from collections import defaultdict
by_model = defaultdict(lambda: {"prompt": 0, "completion": 0, "runs": 0})
for r in runs:
    model = (r.extra or {}).get("invocation_params", {}).get("model_name", "unknown")
    by_model[model]["prompt"] += r.prompt_tokens or 0
    by_model[model]["completion"] += r.completion_tokens or 0
    by_model[model]["runs"] += 1

for model, stats in by_model.items():
    print(f"{model}: {stats['runs']} runs, "
          f"prompt={stats['prompt']:,} tokens, "
          f"completion={stats['completion']:,} tokens")
parameter accepts a structured expression string. This lets you combine multiple conditions without post-processing in Python.

from langsmith import Client

client = Client()

# Runs that received a negative thumbs-down rating
thumbs_down = list(client.list_runs(
    project_name="production",
    filter='and(eq(feedback_key, "user_rating"), eq(feedback_score, 0))',
))

# Runs tagged "experiment-v2" with more than 1000 prompt tokens
expensive_experiments = list(client.list_runs(
    project_name="production",
    filter='and(has(tags, "experiment-v2"), gt(prompt_tokens, 1000))',
))

# Runs that errored on a specific node in a LangGraph chain
graph_errors = list(client.list_runs(
    project_name="production",
    run_type="chain",
    filter='and(has(tags, "agent_node"), neq(error, null))',
))
for membership in arrays (tags, run names);
for combining conditions. All field names and values are case-sensitive.

LangSmith alerts fire when a metric crosses a threshold. Configure them in the LangSmith UI under Settings → Alerts. Each alert monitors one metric in one project over a rolling window and notifies via Slack, email, or a custom webhook.
Error rate alert: trigger when more than 5% of runs fail in a 15-minute window.
Cost alert: trigger when estimated daily cost exceeds a dollar threshold.
Latency alert: trigger when p95 latency exceeds a target SLA (e.g. 10 seconds).
Feedback alert: trigger when mean user_rating score drops below 0.7 in the last hour.

Webhook alerts POST a JSON payload to your endpoint, making it easy to page on-call engineers or open a Jira ticket programmatically. The payload includes the metric value, the threshold, the project name, and a link to the relevant run filter.
LangSmith does not have a native Prometheus or OpenTelemetry exporter, but you can write a small polling script that queries
and pushes metrics to any external system.

# push_to_prometheus.py — run every minute via cron or a background thread
from prometheus_client import Gauge, push_to_gateway
from langsmith import Client
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone

client = Client()
ERROR_RATE = Gauge("langsmith_error_rate", "Fraction of failed runs", ["project"])
P95_LATENCY = Gauge("langsmith_p95_latency_seconds", "p95 chain latency", ["project"])

PROJECT = "production"
cutoff = datetime.now(timezone.utc) - timedelta(minutes=5)

runs = list(client.list_runs(project_name=PROJECT, run_type="chain", start_time=cutoff))
if runs:
    failed = sum(1 for r in runs if r.error)
    ERROR_RATE.labels(project=PROJECT).set(failed / len(runs))

    durations = sorted(
        (r.end_time - r.start_time).total_seconds()
        for r in runs if r.end_time and r.start_time
    )
    if durations:
        P95_LATENCY.labels(project=PROJECT).set(durations[int(len(durations) * 0.95)])

push_to_gateway("localhost:9091", job="langsmith_metrics")
LangGraph graphs emit one trace per
call with child runs for each node. The Monitoring tab aggregates these by the graph's top-level run name. To compare node-level performance, filter by

from langsmith import Client
from collections import defaultdict
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone

client = Client()
cutoff = datetime.now(timezone.utc) - timedelta(hours=1)

# List all child runs (nodes) inside graph executions
node_runs = list(client.list_runs(
    project_name="production",
    run_type="chain",
    start_time=cutoff,
))

# Group by run name (node name in LangGraph)
by_node = defaultdict(list)
for r in node_runs:
    by_node[r.name].append(r)

for node, runs in sorted(by_node.items(), key=lambda x: -len(x[1])):
    durations = [
        (r.end_time - r.start_time).total_seconds()
        for r in runs if r.end_time and r.start_time
    ]
    errors = sum(1 for r in runs if r.error)
    p95 = sorted(durations)[int(len(durations) * 0.95)] if durations else 0
    print(f"{node:30s} calls={len(runs):4d}  errors={errors:3d}  p95={p95:.2f}s")
are a first-class monitoring signal in LangSmith. The Monitoring tab's Feedback chart shows the rolling mean for any feedback key. Use this to detect degradation — a steady drop in
over 24 hours signals a model or prompt regression before error rates or latency change.

from langsmith import Client
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone
from collections import defaultdict

client = Client()
cutoff = datetime.now(timezone.utc) - timedelta(hours=48)

# Fetch all chain run IDs in the last 48h
runs = list(client.list_runs(
    project_name="production",
    run_type="chain",
    start_time=cutoff,
))
run_ids = [str(r.id) for r in runs]

# Fetch user_rating feedback for those runs
feedback = list(client.list_feedback(
    run_ids=run_ids,
    feedback_key="user_rating",
))

# Bucket by hour
by_hour = defaultdict(list)
for fb in feedback:
    if fb.created_at and fb.score is not None:
        hour = fb.created_at.replace(minute=0, second=0, microsecond=0)
        by_hour[hour].append(fb.score)

for hour in sorted(by_hour):
    scores = by_hour[hour]
    mean = sum(scores) / len(scores)
    print(f"{hour.strftime('%Y-%m-%d %H:00')}  mean={mean:.2f}  n={len(scores)}")
In production, logging every single LLM call may generate millions of traces per day. LangSmith supports sampling via the

environment variable (value between 0.0 and 1.0). Only the sampled fraction of runs is sent to LangSmith, reducing both cost and storage while preserving statistical representativeness for monitoring.

import os

# Trace only 10% of runs — set before importing LangChain
os.environ["LANGCHAIN_TRACING_V2"] = "true"
os.environ["LANGCHAIN_API_KEY"] = "ls-..."
os.environ["LANGCHAIN_TRACING_SAMPLING_RATE"] = "0.1"   # 10% sample rate

# All LangChain / LangGraph calls now have 10% tracing probability
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini")
response = llm.invoke("Hello")   # traced ~10% of the time
always trace error runs regardless of the sampling rate by wrapping the call in a try/except and calling
manually for exceptions. This ensures you never miss a failure trace even at low sample rates.
Tag every run with structured metadata fields so you can filter and segment the monitoring dashboard. Useful fields: user cohort, feature flag variant, API version, experiment name.

from langchain_core.runnables import RunnableConfig

# In a FastAPI handler, forward request metadata into the config
async def handle_chat(request: ChatRequest, user: User) -> ChatResponse:
    config = RunnableConfig(
        tags=["production", f"model:{request.model_version}"],
        metadata={
            "user_id": user.id,
            "plan": user.subscription_plan,    # "free" / "pro" / "enterprise"
            "ab_variant": user.ab_group,       # "control" / "treatment"
            "api_version": "v2",
        },
    )
    result = await app.ainvoke(
        {"messages": [{"role": "user", "content": request.message}]},
        config=config,
    )
    return ChatResponse(answer=result["messages"][-1].content)
With this metadata, the LangSmith filter expression
lets you pull only free-tier runs to compare quality and latency against paid-tier runs — without any code changes to the agent itself.
LangSmith does not have built-in ML-based anomaly detection, but you can implement a simple z-score check in a cron job that pages you when a metric drifts significantly from its rolling mean.

# anomaly_check.py — run every 5 minutes
from langsmith import Client
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone
import statistics, sys

client = Client()

def get_p95_latency(minutes: int) -> float:
    cutoff = datetime.now(timezone.utc) - timedelta(minutes=minutes)
    runs = list(client.list_runs(
        project_name="production", run_type="chain", start_time=cutoff))
    durations = sorted(
        (r.end_time - r.start_time).total_seconds()
        for r in runs if r.end_time and r.start_time
    )
    if not durations:
        return 0.0
    return durations[int(len(durations) * 0.95)]

# Compare last-5-min p95 against last-60-min baseline
recent_p95 = get_p95_latency(5)
baseline_p95 = get_p95_latency(60)

if baseline_p95 > 0 and recent_p95 > baseline_p95 * 2.0:
    print(f"ANOMALY: p95 latency {recent_p95:.1f}s vs baseline {baseline_p95:.1f}s")
    # Post alert to Slack, PagerDuty, etc.
    sys.exit(1)

print(f"OK: p95={recent_p95:.1f}s baseline={baseline_p95:.1f}s")
LangSmith Monitoring aggregates latency, token usage, cost, and error rate automatically from traces — no extra instrumentation needed.
The Monitoring tab's time-series charts link directly to the underlying runs so you can drill into any spike.
client.list_runs() with filter expressions pulls any production slice into Python for custom aggregation or export.
Supported filter operators: eq/neq/gt/lt/gte/lte for comparisons, has() for tag membership, and/or/not for combining.
Alerts are configured in the LangSmith UI and can fire to Slack, email, or a webhook when a metric crosses a threshold.
For LangGraph agents, child runs for each node appear under the graph's top-level trace — group by run.name to get per-node stats.
Export metrics to Prometheus, Grafana, or Datadog by polling list_runs() in a background script and pushing gauge values.
